Black History Traced in Mount Show in Setauket
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When you belong to a lot of organizations, you need a lot of briefcases," said Ted Green, as he leafed through papers on his roots from his home In Setauket. 

     Mr. Green, a retired construction foreman and the historian of the Hart and Sells families, has traced his family to the 1600's, when the Seetalcott Indians, for whom the area is named, intermarried with other tribes and blacks who came to the area or were brought on slave ships. Most of his neighbors are relatives, said Mr. Green, adding that he believed his family was the oldest in  Setauket.

ON THE MAP;Trying to Save a Piece of Black History in Lawnside
nytimes_articles.abstract:
As early as 1792, just two years after slavery was outlawed in New Jersey, an African-American community had coalesced at a place called Snow Hill. The town,  one of the first in the United States to be organized and governed by free blacks, is now known as the borough of Lawnside. 

     Peter Mott, minister of the community's African Methodist Episcopal church in the mid-1800's, is believed to have used his home, built around 1845, as a haven on the Underground Railroad for slaves seeking freedom.

BACKTALK;Knee Lifts, Laughter and a Better Life
nytimes_articles.abstract:
On Jan. 15, Fay Gallant, one of my trainee-athletes, had her 92d birthday, all of which had been celebrated in the United States except her first, which had been in Czarist Russia. 

     After a stop at Ellis Island, her family settled in the Brownsville section of Brooklyn, where she graduated from the eighth grade before she went to work in the garment sweatshops, married, had two children and became the matriarch for a large extended family that lived together in a third-floor walkup.

NEW YORKERS & CO.
STYLE;Chewing the Fat
nytimes_articles.abstract:
For a person who has flirted with anorexia, it is a pleasure to see men of a  certain age worrying about their weight. Listening to them is not such a pleasure. Because the men are new at the game, they don't hesitate to discuss the fat problem. Incessantly. Women of the same age do not discuss the fat problem, especially not in mixed company. They prefer to face the problem with quiet dignity. Discussing the problem might only draw attention to some stray body part that may be successfully tucked away under an article of clothing. 

     The age at which a man begins to explore the fat problem can vary. The actual problem can manifest itself in the early 30's, but broad-range discussion usually starts later. There are early nonverbal symptoms. I've watched the rugged journalist who shares my apartment sneak by with a Diet Coke. His shirts  are no longer neatly tucked in to display a trim waist. Recently he has begun to verbalize his anxiety. He tells me, with a sheepish grin, that he is taking his  suits to Chinatown to have them "tailored."

La Forza del Destino': Verdi at His Gloomiest And, So, Most Verdian
nytimes_articles.abstract:
If there is such a thing as the "most Verdian" of Verdi's operas -- Verdi being, perhaps, the gloomiest of great opera composers -- a good case can be made for "La Forza del Destino." This dark, sweeping epic, which once held a place of honor in the Metropolitan Opera repertory, returns for the first time since 1984 on Thursday, in a new production directed by Giancarlo del Monaco and featuring Sharon Sweet, Placido Domingo and Vladimir Chernov. 

     Until the general revaluation of Verdi's work in recent decades, "Forza" got  little respect from commentators. But it was known to be a prime specimen of the Romantic grand opera that blossomed in the presence of big, healthy Italian-style voices. Such voices were once the Met's stock in trade, and for its first "Forza," in 1918, the company was better equipped than perhaps even it knew.

YOUR TAXES;Filing Electronically Remains for the Few
nytimes_articles.abstract:
ONE day in the not-so-distant future the traditional midnight run to the downtown post office on April 15 will be replaced by a surge in cyberspace as computers transmit tax returns electronically. 

     For now, however, despite the advertising hype of tax services, electronic filing remains the domain of a minority of taxpayers in a hurry to a receive their refunds -- and who in many cases rely on high-interest loans against their refunds just to pay their tax preparers' fees. For the majority of middle- or high-income taxpayers, or anyone owing money to the Internal Revenue Service, filing a tax return electronically is either impractical or impossible.

SOAPBOX;Starlight Over 10th Avenue
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I'M one of Manhattan's lucky ones. I have a view. From my fifth-floor walk-up on 36th Street I can look down along 10th Avenue, and up toward the southern sky. 

     It's not the view I started with when I moved in 20 years ago. Back then, I could step out onto my summer porch, the fire escape, and see the Hudson River off to the west. On weekends I would watch as the cruise ships made their way down the river, their stately progress accompanied by the blasting of whistles.  Later, a massive billboard went up on 10th Avenue, slicing off the bottom third  of my southern view.

Prizing Ski Runs in the Northwest
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I FIRST learned about Northwest skiing several years ago, when I moved from Chicago to Washington State. While hauling my life across the country, I began to pick out scores of little red skiers -- the symbol for ski resorts -- on the  Rand McNally road atlas. 

     Every winter since, I've packed my car with ski equipment, intent on finding  the best slopes the region has to offer. Of the more than 100 ski resorts that dot the Cascades and northern Rockies, several offer abundant terrain, short or  nonexistent lift lines, and a good range of accommodations, all at a reasonable  price.

MAKING IT WORK;Floating in Limbo
nytimes_articles.abstract:
CAPT. SRETO PETROVIC'S hands are stuffed into the pockets of his brown sports coat, and, standing on the deck of his cargo vessel docked at Red Hook, he looks cold. His face is covered with a lively brown shrub of facial hair and his eyes  seem tired even though he hasn't done much of anything today. 

     "It's better here," the 43-year-old seafarer says. "We are not on duty. We are usually in our cabins or the television room."

Now, Forager
nytimes_articles.abstract: I never buy anything new if I can help it. My wardrobe and my house have been furnished almost exclusively from thrift, junk and consignment shops. I've been  thrifting for decades, so I no longer need much and I mostly just upgrade and deaccession. To keep my reflexes in shape, I will often go thrifting theoretically -- moneyless, with an imaginary character to style -- which is like practicing on a silent keyboard. In my spare time, I also volunteer as a guide for the style-impaired. Recently, at the Council Thrift Shop, I put together a new wardrobe for a total stranger. She was very surprised to discover I didn't work there.

LIVES;Formerly Anonymous
nytimes_articles.abstract:
For months now, I have been researching a new book (not a political novel) at a small library on the East Side, where, weekly, I've exchanged pleasantries with the librarians at the front desk. The library actually owns books I've written. I've never been greeted by name. Last week I was greeted by name. 

     "I've been hearing about you," the librarian said.

More Fathers Are Choosing To Become At-Home Dads
nytimes_articles.abstract:
JOHN CLEMENTS, 28, of Bellmore cooks, cleans and spends weekdays caring for his infant daughter while his wife works 11-hour days as an accountant. But he would prefer if you didn't call him "Mr. Mom." 

     "While I'm not really offended by it, I do think I'm a step above where Mr. Mom was," he said. "He was basically incompetent and a loser.  He got fired from his job and then at home he would burn the food and explode the vacuum cleaner. I don't do that."

FROM THE DESK OF: VALERIE FOLEY;No Credit Where It Is Due
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IF there is any other woman on the face of the planet who has ever had an income exceeding the old standard of $1,000 for each year of age, more than a year's salary in savings and an excess of $10,000 in material assets with no outstanding debt since 1980, but was still unable to get a bank to trust her with a credit card, I would like to meet her to commiserate. 

     In 1977, I did get a credit card at a department store where I worked. For purchases elsewhere, I relied on my husband's Mastercharge (the predecessor to Mastercard), which he had received unsolicited during the early heady days of charge-card expansion.

nytimes_articles.abstract: THE LOVE AFFAIR AS A WORK OF ART By Dan Hofstadter. 314 pp. New York: Farrar, Straus & Giroux. $24.    WILL the suggestive title of this witty and wise book be understood? In our age of sexual promiscuity and quantified orgasms, readers may expect an essay on skillful preludes and artful caresses. First the telephone, then the fax and now E-mail have made the handwritten love letter, with its sensuous and emotional associations, an archaic experience. In "The Love Affair as a Work of Art," Dan  Hofstadter evokes a 19th-century world in which love letters were an art form with a narrative potential. His book, more than merely nostalgic, raises questions about issues ranging from the sincerity of feelings to the vexing relation between art and life.

DINING OUT;Chicken Stands Out at New Steakhouse
nytimes_articles.abstract:
TWO years ago a steakhouse replacing an Italian restaurant was news. Now it is not. It recently happened again in Woodmere when G. Roberts Steakhouse opened in the double storefront quarters on Broadway that once housed a succession of casual Italian cafes. 

     The open, airy cafe look remains. New features include an etched-glass room divider emblazoned with the restaurant's name, and creamy walls dotted with bright artwork, available at the nearby Owl 57 Galleries.

New York Disclosure Law Snares a Schools Chief
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Everyone agrees that Charles P. Skiptunas did the right thing when he forced  a teacher he suspected of fondling a student to resign. 

     But State Education Department officials said Mr. Skiptunas, the Superintendent for the Middletown School District, did not take the next crucial step and notify New York State of his suspicions about the teacher's conduct, as required by law. As a result, state officials say, Mr. Skiptunas put the teen-age girl and possibly other children at risk.

Mexican Hot Chocolate
nytimes_articles.abstract: Mexican chocolate is available at Mexican markets, some grocery stores and always online. Often you will find it under the brand name Ibarra, in a distinct yellow- and red-striped, hexagon-shaped box. Ibarra chocolate comes in both dark chocolate and in ''new'' flavorings — mixed with sugar cane, cinnamon and finely ground almonds. Get the former and follow the lead of our recipe’s source, Rick Bayless, the great American interpreter of Mexican cuisine.

nytimes_articles.headline: Rumplemayer’s Hot Chocolate
nytimes_articles.abstract: This version, adapted from Rumplemayer's, a now-closed New York restaurant once known for its hot chocolate and pastries, is rich, sweet and smooth. It calls for real semisweet chocolate (we bet a mix of semisweet and bittersweet would be delightful, too) so there's none of the chalky aftertaste hot cocoa often leaves behind. A dollop of whipped cream and a sprinkle of cinnamon, if you wish, take it over the top.

Positively Negative
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Every election year sees an increase in negative political advertising, and every year the pundits and commentators tell us that the voters are fed up with  it and want more positive discussion of the issues. The talking heads are wrong. Negative ads not only work, they give voters better information than positive ads. 

     Negative advertising has always been with us. Thomas Jefferson's detractors conducted a whispering campaign regarding his relationship with a female slave.  Abraham Lincoln's adversaries distributed handbills claiming he had fathered an  illegitimate child. Opponents of Grover Cleveland, who had fathered a child out  of wedlock, posted banners with the legend: "Ma Ma, where's my Pa? Gone to the White House! Ha! Ha! Ha!"
